questions.question: You hash 1,000 distinct items into a hash table of size 999. By the pigeonhole principle, what does that guarantee?
options: At least one collision (some bucket holds ≥ 2 items)
questions.explanation: Pigeonhole guarantees existence: 1000 items into 999 boxes → some box has ≥ 2. It doesn't tell you which bucket or how many — only that at least one collision is unavoidable. This is the foundation of birthday-paradox-style attacks on n-bit hashes.

questions.question: Inclusion-exclusion for two events: |A ∪ B| = ?
questions.explanation: |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| - |A ∩ B|. The naïve sum |A| + |B| double-counts elements in both; subtract |A ∩ B| once to correct. For three sets, you also add back the triple intersection — sign alternates.

questions.question: Catalan numbers Cₙ count balanced parentheses, binary trees, lattice paths under the diagonal, and many other objects. Why are all those counts equal?
options: Each object can be put in bijection with the next — a constructive recipe maps one to another. Equality of counts follows from existence of bijections.
They all satisfy the same recurrence Cₙ = Σ Cₖ · Cₙ₋₁₋ₖ — and recurrences with the same base case have the same solution.
questions.explanation: Both: each object class admits the same recursive decomposition (split-at-pivot), giving the same recurrence and base case → same numbers. Additionally, explicit bijections (paren ↔ tree ↔ lattice path) prove the equality structurally. This is the essence of bijective combinatorics.

questions.question: Generalized pigeonhole: 100 items into 30 boxes. Some box must contain at least how many?
questions.explanation: ⌈n/k⌉ is the floor on the largest box. ⌈100/30⌉ = 4. Existence-only — you don't know which box, and at most one box achieves it (the others might have 3). Pigeonhole gives lower bounds, not upper.

questions.question: How many integers in [1, 100] are divisible by NONE of {2, 3, 5}?
options: Both A and C — they're equal
questions.explanation: Inclusion-exclusion gives |div 2 ∪ div 3 ∪ div 5| = 74. The complement is 100 - 74 = 26 numbers divisible by none. (Quick check: these are 1, 7, 11, 13, 17, …, 97 — coprime-to-30 in [1,100].)

questions.question: Why is C₄ = 14? Quick interpretation:
options: 14 ways to parenthesize four pairs of brackets: ()()()(),  ()()(()),  ()(())(),  ((()))(),  etc.
14 distinct binary trees with 4 internal nodes
14 lattice paths from (0,0) to (4,4) staying below the diagonal
questions.explanation: C₄ = 14 — that's the count of every one of those objects (and many more). Stanley's book lists 200+ classes of objects whose count is Cₙ; all in bijection with one another. C₄'s 14 parenthesis strings are exactly the 14 binary trees of 4 nodes via the recursive split bijection.
